lesson_objective: A for loop replaces repeated, near-identical lines of code with one controlled repetition, running exactly as many times as you tell it to — no more, no less.
lesson_summary: This lesson opens with the pain of copy-pasted print statements, introduces the for loop as the fix, and immediately shows the off-by-one trap that comes with controlling your own loop bounds.
key_points: Repeating a line five times by hand doesn't scale and invites copy-paste mistakes.
A for loop has three parts: a starting point, a continue condition, and an update step.
The loop runs exactly as many times as the condition allows — getting the boundary wrong is the most common bug.
step_by_step_explanation: Open on five nearly-identical System.out.println lines printing numbers 1 through 5 — the tedious, error-prone way.
Rewrite it as a single for loop and show the same output with one line of logic.
Break down the loop's three parts: initialization, condition, update — in one breath, on screen.
Introduce the off-by-one trap: change < to <= and show one extra, unintended iteration.
Land on the takeaway: the loop runs exactly as many times as its condition says — read that condition carefully.

core_example.description: Five duplicated print statements rewritten as a single for loop, then deliberately broken with an off-by-one change.
core_example.focus_note: Highlight the condition i <= 5 specifically when introducing the off-by-one trap — swap to i < 5 on screen to show the visible difference in output.

analogy: A for loop is like giving someone exact directions to a shelf: start here, keep going while this is true, move this much each step — miss one part of the instructions and they stop in the wrong place.
common_mistakes: Off-by-one errors from an incorrect loop bound (< vs <=).
Copy-pasting near-identical lines instead of recognizing a repeatable pattern.
what_to_avoid_saying: Do not say a for loop is 'always' the right choice over a while loop — it's specifically suited to a known number of iterations; that contrast belongs to the While Loops lesson, not this one.
beginner_takeaway: A for loop runs exactly as many times as its condition allows — read the condition, don't assume it.
retention_hook: You've typed the same line five times in a row. There's a one-line fix, and it comes with exactly one trap.
visual_notes: Show the five duplicated print lines fading into the single for loop as the rewrite happens.
Freeze-frame on the loop's three-part header (init; condition; update) with each part labeled.
